ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Nasi Rawon. A specific Indonesian rice-and-beef-soup composite entry is absent from the FoodOn ontology as of 2024. Null retained to preserve ontology honesty.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef soup — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 analogue for the rawon broth component of this rice-and-beef-soup composite dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Nasi Rawon. A0FJP (beef soup) is the closest defensible parent anchor. The rice component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nasi Rawon or its direct parent category in the clinical food hierarchy as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 plate / bowl (approximately 400 g including rice and broth)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Nasi Rawon is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice accompanied by a portion of black beef broth with tender beef pieces, typically garnished with bean sprouts, salted duck egg, and sambal on the side.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nasi Rawon / Beef Soup with Rice composite; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef soup and cooked white rice entries for micronutrient cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB or USDA entry exactly matches Nasi Rawon as a composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo beef-based rice dish entries and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878) and beef soup (FDC ID 172945). Micronutrient values for buah keluak contribution are informed by published food chemistry literature (Food Chemistry journal, 2024). The input record values (energy 125 kcal, protein 9.5 g, fat 7.8 g, carbohydrate 4.2 g per 100 g) are consistent with a rice-and-beef-soup composite at approximately 60:40 rice-to-broth ratio and are retained as the best available estimate. Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g per 100 g based on USDA beef soup fatty acid profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on beef and rice composite dish; University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed rice-and-protein dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Rawon in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated based on the analogous profile of a mixed rice-and-beef-soup dish: steamed white rice (GI ~72) diluted by a high-protein, high-fat beef broth component that substantially lowers the effective GI of the composite meal. The protein and fat content (9.5 g and 7.8 g per 100 g respectively) are well-established GI-lowering factors per Jenkins et al. (1981, Am J Clin Nutr) and Wolever et al. (1994). Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (45 × 16.8 g total carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 7.6, where total carbohydrate per serving = 4.2 g/100 g × 400 g = 16.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g; approximately 1520 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Nasi Rawon to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt broth preparation, omit or halve the salted duck egg accompaniment, and monitor total daily sodium intake to remain within the WHO guideline of 2000 mg per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~45) due to high protein and fat content, but rice portion contributes meaningful carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may include Nasi Rawon in a controlled meal plan by moderating the rice portion to 100–150 g cooked rice, prioritising the protein-rich beef broth, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (380 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) from beef and cooking fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving frequency to once or twice per week, choose lean beef cuts, request low-sodium broth, and omit the salted duck egg to reduce combined sodium and dietary cholesterol burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) are beneficial, but high sodium and red meat may be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric and ginger in the spice paste provide curcumin and gingerol with documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should moderate red meat intake and limit sodium to reduce systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Rawon is a traditional Javanese beef soup dish served with steamed white rice, originating from East Java, Indonesia, and widely consumed across the Malay archipelago. Its most distinctive characteristic is its jet-black broth, produced by the addition of buah keluak (black nuts from the Pangium edule tree), which impart a deep, earthy, and mildly nutty flavour. The beef is slow-cooked until tender in a complex spice paste comprising lemongrass, galangal, ginger, turmeric, shallots, garlic, and kaffir lime leaves. The dish is traditionally served with steamed white rice, a salted duck egg, fresh bean sprouts, and a spicy sambal on the side. Texturally, the broth is rich and slightly thick, the beef is fork-tender, and the bean sprouts provide a fresh crunch. Nutritionally, Nasi Rawon is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with relatively low carbohydrate content per 100 g (protein 9.5 g, fat 7.8 g, carbohydrate 4.2 g, energy 125 kcal per 100 g), making it a nutritionally dense meal. The sodium content is high at 380 mg per 100 g, primarily from the salted duck egg and seasoned broth, which is a clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 (low) reflects the moderating effect of the high protein and fat content on postprandial glucose response, making it a reasonable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when rice portions are controlled. The dish is halal-certified in most commercial preparations and is gluten-free and dairy-free by default. It is culturally significant as one of the oldest recorded dishes in Javanese culinary heritage and is commonly served at lunch and dinner.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi rawon?
Is nasi rawon good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact entry for Nasi Rawon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB beef-based rice dish entries and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy beef soup and cooked white rice entries. The input record values are internally consistent with the described dish composition and are retained as the best available estimate. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Nasi Rawon as a composite dish are not available in HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of April 2026. These fields require direct laboratory analysis of a standardised recipe to populate accurately. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g per 100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy beef soup fatty acid profile (FDC ID 172945); this is an adapted estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 3. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Nasi Rawon exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 4. The input record cited 'Food Chemistry' (sciencedirect.com) as a source; this is a peer-reviewed journal and is acceptable as supplementary evidence for buah keluak nutritional properties but is not an approved primary nutrient database source under Project Veritas v2 policy. Primary nutrient values are therefore attributed to HPB/USDA as described above. 5. The input record GI value of 35 has been revised upward to 45 to better reflect the rice component contribution to the composite dish GI; a GI of 35 is more consistent with a pure protein/fat food and is not defensible for a rice-inclusive dish. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × total carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (45 × 16.8) / 100 = 7.6 g, where total carbohydrate per serving = 4.2 g/100 g × 400 g = 16.8 g. 7. foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'analogous_estimate') to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.

Updated foodex2_gap_note to reflect 'closest_parent' terminology consistently.
